Apprentice's Phial-Belt.name: Apprentice's Phial-Belt
Apprentice's Phial-Belt.description: [Common] A plain leather strap with a single stitched cup at the hip for one glass phial. The first carry given to a witch's apprentice on the day she is trusted to fill her own. Useless to anyone untrained in the craft.

Witch's Travel Bandolier.name: Witch's Travel Bandolier
Witch's Travel Bandolier.description: [Uncommon] A double-stitched leather bandolier with three padded loops across the chest, corks waxed against hard riding. The working carry of a witch on the road — three days of mana kept warm against the ribs. To another witch the rig reads as a colleague filled honestly; to an outsider it is strange tackle.

Master Witch's Phial-Harness.name: Master Witch's Phial-Harness
Master Witch's Phial-Harness.description: [Rare] A fitted vest-harness of fine kid leather, deep-dyed red, with five blown-glass phials seated in silver-mouthed cradles across the ribs. Issued to a witch judged fit to travel under formal warrant, the cradles damped against falls and the phials machine-true. In a city that knows the craft she moves like a guild-master; on the road she keeps it under a cloak.

Senior Witch's Filling Harness.name: Senior Witch's Filling Harness
Senior Witch's Filling Harness.description: [Epic] A double-bandolier crossing both shoulders — seven phials across the front and an eighth at the small of the back where the hand falls naturally during a working. The phials are clear blown crystal ground to identical bore, fill rates predictable to the minute. A senior witch wearing this rig can draw down three partners in an evening, stopper the lot, and open a working at dawn with everything she needs.

Mistress-Harness of the Tenth Phial.name: Mistress-Harness of the Tenth Phial
Mistress-Harness of the Tenth Phial.description: [Legendary] A full body harness of black kid leather suspended from a shoulder yoke, ten phials in matched cradles across two crossed sashes — each a hand-blown reservoir capable of holding a full working between its walls. The pattern is attributed to a pre-Ensoulment leatherer remembered only as the Mother of Phials, and harnesses cut to her line are vanishingly rare. The yoke fits one witch's body alone; another woman wearing it feels the previous bearer still in the leather.

Apprentice's Apothecary Satchel.name: Apprentice's Apothecary Satchel
Apprentice's Apothecary Satchel.description: [Common] A canvas satchel divided inside by stitched cloth into a dozen narrow pockets for vials, packets of dried leaf, and a small brass mortar. Smells of camphor, dried mint, and the faint sourness of fermented honey-wine. The first kit handed to an apprentice on the day she is trusted to draw a salve unsupervised.

Journeyman Apothecary's Kit.name: Journeyman Apothecary's Kit
Journeyman Apothecary's Kit.description: [Uncommon] A hardened leather satchel with a fold-out wooden inner frame, racks for sixteen labeled vials, a folding brass scale, a pestle, two sealed pots of base reagent, and a slim journeyman's manual in the bearer's own hand. The shoulder-strap is stained dark with years of handling. Carried by a working apothecary who takes posts on the road.

Master Apothecary's Field Case.name: Master Apothecary's Field Case
Master Apothecary's Field Case.description: [Rare] A polished campaign case of cherry-wood and tooled leather, hinged to open into three tiered trays — thirty-two graduated vials, a folding alembic, three calibrated scales, a sealed reagent block, and the master's working notebook in its waxed wallet. The brass clasps bear the apothecary's personal mark; at any trade-house gate the case is a recognized credential.

Guild-Master's Apothecary Case.name: Guild-Master's Apothecary Case
Guild-Master's Apothecary Case.description: [Epic] A wide flat case of black walnut bound in oxblood leather, the corners reinforced in worked silver. The inner frame opens to five tiers: sixty graduated vials in spring-loaded racks, a full folding distillation rig, six matched scales, a sealed cabinet of rare reagents, and the master's grimoire — the working notes of a lifetime, bound in calf and worth more than the case itself. To pass through a city gate with this case open on a porter's cart is to declare oneself, and most guild-masters do exactly that.

The Vask Reagent-Case.name: The Vask Reagent-Case
The Vask Reagent-Case.description: [Legendary] A small flat tray of rosewood and silver wire, ninety-six tiny phials racked in a Wartide-era pattern attributed to the apothecary-master Vask, whose reagent tables still shape the continental trade six centuries on. Three pages of brittle working-notes are folded in the back, the script private and tight, never fully deciphered. To a working apothecary the rack-pattern reads like a sentence already half-spoken; to anyone else it is the most expensive box of empty bottles on the continent.

Tooth-Charm of the Line.name: Tooth-Charm of the Line
Tooth-Charm of the Line.description: [Common] A short ironwood baton banded in copper, a single ancestor's tooth set into the head with bone-paste and red wax. The first working relic given to a novice ancestor-mage on the day they leave the bone-vault unsupervised. The tooth is named, and the novice has been taught the name; in any other hand it is a stick with a tooth in it.

Novice's Bone-Wand.name: Novice's Bone-Wand
Novice's Bone-Wand.description: [Uncommon] A short white wand cut from a tibia, polished smooth and bound at the handle in plaited blue cord. Three named teeth sit in a ring at the head, each chosen for the kind of working it strengthens. Carried by an ancestor-mage on the first posting beyond the cloister; the working passes through ancestor and bearer both, and no untrained hand can open the channel.

Mage's Lineage-Rod.name: Mage's Lineage-Rod
Mage's Lineage-Rod.description: [Rare] A working-rod the length of an arm, the shaft a femur taken four generations back from a forebear who consented to the use at the deathbed rite. The bone is dressed in plaited indigo cord, capped at the foot in worked silver, and the head set with a clenched fist of seven named teeth. The visible mark of an ancestor-mage of full standing — a relic-keeper who has earned vault-access.

Elder-Mother's Reliquary Staff.name: Elder-Mother's Reliquary Staff
Elder-Mother's Reliquary Staff.description: [Epic] Twelve feet of paired long-bones lashed in plaited indigo and crimson cord, crowned with a worked silver cap holding a prepared elder jawbone — every tooth named, every tooth woken. The work of a cloister bone-wright over a year of preparation rites and assigned only to a senior ancestor-mage seated on a high council. To carry one through a kindred hold is to be greeted by every novice on the road as kin. Outsiders see a tall white staff with too many teeth and edge sideways.

The Long-Bone of Vaerith Eight-Mothers.name: The Long-Bone of Vaerith Eight-Mothers
The Long-Bone of Vaerith Eight-Mothers.description: [Legendary] Fourteen feet of paired femur lashed in cord spliced and replaced a hundred times since the pre-Ensoulment age, the original splice still visible at the head. The silver cap holds the jawbone of Vaerith Eight-Mothers, a Prime elder remembered only in fragments of the oldest cloister-chants, whose eight maternal lines are said to braid through nearly every living ancestor-mage of the kindred. The staff answers by descent, not by training; a bearer truly of her blood lifts it correctly and feels centuries of trained ancestor-work crowd the inside of the skull and become, for a few breaths, hers to use.

Apprentice's Sword.name: Apprentice's Sword
Apprentice's Sword.description: [Common] A one-handed cut-and-thrust blade of plain steel, hilt wrapped in undyed leather, crossguard a simple straight bar. Town-smith work for the muster-purse; balance is honest, the edge takes a stone, the point holds true. The off-hand stays free for shield, parrying dagger, or buckler.

Town-Smith's Blade.name: Town-Smith's Blade
Town-Smith's Blade.description: [Uncommon] A one-handed arming sword from a market-town trade-smith. The steel is folded and the temper holds; grip wrapped in waxed leather over braided wire, crossguard worked with the smith's mark. The sword a working man-at-arms buys with his first season's pay and carries until he earns better. Off-hand remains free for shield or parrying gear.

Sergeant's Steel.name: Sergeant's Steel
Sergeant's Steel.description: [Rare] A one-handed war-sword in the household-sergeant pattern — pattern-welded steel dark and bright in the polish, grip wrapped in dyed kid over silver wire, the pommel a heavy worked octagon, the crossguard chased with a unit badge. The blade a veteran of ten years' service carries, given by a captain who recognized the work. Other men-at-arms in a hall see this sword and know they are looking at someone who has fought and lived through it.

Captain's Blade of the Marches.name: Captain's Blade of the Marches
Captain's Blade of the Marches.description: [Epic] A one-handed war-sword of master-grade work, the blade fullered for lightness and pattern-welded in the close style favored by senior marches captains. The fittings are silver-gilt over steel, the grip wrapped in dyed shark-hide bound with twisted silver wire, the pommel worked into the head of a stooping falcon. Earned, not bought: a captain receives one on the day they take command of a marches company, and the ricasso carries the etched names of the previous bearers.

Heart's-Reach, the Long Answer.name: Heart's-Reach, the Long Answer
Heart's-Reach, the Long Answer.description: [Legendary] A named one-handed war-sword of high pre-modern pattern, attributed to a Wartide-era smith remembered as Vrast the Quiet, whose surviving blades number in single digits. The steel takes a polish that does not dull and an edge that turns aside other steel without notching; the crossguard is wrought in twin reaching hands of worked silver, the pommel a closed fist holding a single drop of garnet. The grip has been rewrapped many times in two centuries, but the original silver wire still lies set into the wood beneath. A fighter lifting it feels the weight settle exactly correct and understands, suddenly, why old men gave it a name.

Apprentice's Self-Bow.name: Apprentice's Self-Bow
Apprentice's Self-Bow.description: [Common] A single-stave self-bow of seasoned ash, draw weight modest, string of waxed linen. The first bow a back-country apprentice cuts under a hunter's eye, finished with rasp and tallow on the night of the first frost. Requires both hands throughout the shot; no other weapon may be carried in the off-hand while the bow is wielded.

Hunter's Longbow.name: Hunter's Longbow
Hunter's Longbow.description: [Uncommon] A working longbow of yew or hickory in the back-country pattern — six feet of seasoned stave, back painted with pine pitch against damp, horn nocks at tip and bottom, string of waxed sinew kept in an oiled pouch when not strung. Built by a country bowyer for a hunter who has earned the price. Both hands required throughout; the off-hand grips the bow and cannot hold any other weapon. Brings down deer at a hundred paces and bandits at fifty.

Marches-Bow of the Long Patrol.name: Marches-Bow of the Long Patrol
Marches-Bow of the Long Patrol.description: [Rare] A composite recurve of horn, sinew, and dark-stained core wood, built to the pattern carried by long-patrol skirmishers along the broken-country borders. Heavier draw than a self-bow, faster cast, the limbs glued and seasoned over eighteen months in a master bowyer's loft. The recurve profile reads at a hundred yards — a patrol veteran with one of these slung at the shoulder is unmistakable. Both hands required; the off-hand keeps grip on the bow throughout the shot and cannot hold any other weapon.

Master-Fletched Hunter's Recurve.name: Master-Fletched Hunter's Recurve
Master-Fletched Hunter's Recurve.description: [Epic] A master-built recurve in the high hunter's pattern — composite horn-and-sinew limbs over a core of dark heart-yew, grip wrapped in dyed snake-skin and bound in silver wire at the throat. Comes paired with a matched quiver and a brace of master-fletched arrows; the bowyer's mark is burned into the inside of the lower limb. To own one is either to be rich enough to pay a master bowyer's price or hunter enough that one of them took an interest. Both hands required throughout; the off-hand cannot be filled while the bow is wielded.

Hessa's Last Recurve, called Quiet-in-th.name: Hessa's Last Recurve, called Quiet-in-the-Reeds
Hessa's Last Recurve, called Quiet-in-th.description: [Legendary] The last bow attributed to Hessa, a bowyer of the pre-Ensoulment age remembered now only by her name and the dozen surviving works of her hand. The limbs are composite — horn, sinew, and a core of heart-yew so cleanly seamed that no eye finds the join — the grip still wrapped in the original waxed linen Hessa is said to have laid down in her own workroom. The bow has passed through unbroken chains of quiet bequest for centuries; no holder has ever sold it. A hunter drawing it at full extension hears the limbs sigh and understands, suddenly, that the bow has chosen to be held.
